Condominio El Oasis · Playa Hermosa · Guanacaste

Garden Field Log

Every plant we choose either adds to this web or doesn’t. These are the transactions — visitor, host, behavior — recorded in the garden across the seasons.

32Interactions documented
5Visitor spp. on Coatbuttons
4Visitor spp. on Duranta
3Butterfly spp. on Ixora
2Trophic levels on Cashew
Dry Season · January – May
#99
January
Cracker butterfly
on Yellow flowering shrub
Resting on petal
Wings closed, red hindwing spots visible. Perched on the face of a petal rather than the disc — resting, not nectaring. Backlighting through yellow petals.
Hamadryas sp. / Consul fabius · Unidentified
#995
January
Stingless bee
on Duranta / Sky Flower
In-flight approach — departing with pollen
Bee suspended mid-flight between Duranta flowers, pollen visible on hind legs. The iridescent wings and orange body are characteristic of several Meliponini genera. Third visitor guild documented on Duranta — after the paper wasp and the Tropical Buckeye. January, dry season.
Duranta · 4 visitor spp. recorded
Meliponini sp. · Duranta erecta
#997
January
Streak-backed Oriole
on Date palm — flower/fruit spike
Foraging on inflorescence
The oriole appears here on the date palm inflorescence — cream-colored flower and early fruit clusters — three months before its record on the cashew apple. Two plant species, same bird. The palm is also documented with the Hoffmann's Woodpecker feeding on ripe drupes in December. The date palm is becoming one of the most-visited plants in the log.
Streak-backed Oriole · 2 plant spp.
Icterus pustulatus · Phoenix sp.
#999
February
Stingless bee
on Turnera / White Alder
Nectaring at flower center
Small bee working the center of a Turnera subulata flower — the white petals and crimson-purple central blotch are unmistakable. Turnera is a prolific dry-season bloomer and a significant meliponine forage plant. The bee is positioned directly on the anthers; pollen transfer is near-certain.
Meliponini sp. · Turnera subulata
#001
April
Sulphur butterfly
on Red hibiscus
Nectaring inside flower
Pale yellow butterfly working deep inside the flower for nectar. The scale of the butterfly relative to the bloom makes the dependency legible at a glance.
Phoebis sp. · Hibiscus rosa-sinensis
#101
April
Red-lored Amazon
on Cashew
Eating immature nut — seed predation
The parrot is holding and excavating an immature cashew nut directly in the tree. Unlike the Streak-backed Oriole feeding on the ripe hypocarp, this is seed predation — the nut is destroyed in the process, not dispersed. Two birds, same plant, opposite ecological roles. April, dry season.
Cashew · 2 bird spp., opposite roles
Amazona autumnalis · Anacardium occidentale
#100
April
Variegated squirrel
on Papaya
Feeding on fallen fruit
Ground-level foraging at tree base. Fallen papaya braced against roots, orange flesh exposed. Most wildlife photography misses this layer — what happens after fruit falls.
Sciurus variegatoides · Carica papaya
#007
May
Orange-barred Sulphur
on Ixora
Nectaring, post-rain
Large pale green-yellow sulphur working the red Ixora cluster. Water droplets on the flowers place this in the first rains of May. Ixora is among the highest-traffic plants in the garden — this is its first documented visitor in this log.
Ixora · 3 butterfly spp. recorded
Phoebis philea · Ixora coccinea
#004
May
Great-tailed Grackle
on Palm — immature fruit cluster
Foraging on immature drupes
Male grackle — glossy iridescent black, pale iris, bill open — perched among green immature palm fruits. Grackles are highly opportunistic omnivores, working fruit clusters for whatever is accessible. May, transitional season.
Quiscalus mexicanus · Arecaceae sp.
#017
May
Skipper butterfly
on Pink flower — post-rain
Nectaring
Stocky brown skipper with hooked antennae characteristic of the Hesperiidae, nectaring on a four-petaled pink flower still carrying rain droplets. Skippers are fast and often overlooked; this one held long enough for a full frame. May, first rains.
Hesperiidae sp. · Unidentified (possibly Cuphea or Tibouchina)
#002
May
Tropical Buckeye
on Duranta / Sky Flower
Nectaring
Brown butterfly with bold eyespots and orange banding on the lavender flowers of Duranta. The eyespots are a predator-deflection adaptation — the garden is not a safe place even here.
Duranta · 4 visitor spp. recorded
Junonia evarete · Duranta erecta
Wet Season · June – September
#987
June
Gulf Fritillary
on Pride of Barbados
Nectaring
Orange-brown wings with silver-white spots on underside — the Gulf Fritillary is unmistakable. Nectaring on Pride of Barbados, whose red-and-yellow flowers are engineered for large-bodied visitors. June, early wet season. A striking pairing — both the butterfly and the plant are among the most visually arresting in the garden.
Agraulis vanillae · Caesalpinia pulcherrima
#876
June
Banded butterfly
on Duranta / Sky Flower
Nectaring, wings spread
Dark butterfly with cream bands and red hindwing spots on Duranta, wings held open — basking or nectaring. Fourth visitor species documented on this plant: paper wasp, Tropical Buckeye, stingless bee, and now this. The Duranta is earning its place on the inventory.
Duranta · 4 visitor spp. recorded
Consul fabius / Hamadryas sp. · Duranta erecta
#200
August
Stingless bees
on Piper spike
Resin foraging, post-rain
Three meliponine bees on an immature Piper infructescence in the rain. Stingless bees collect plant resins for nest construction — this is not pollination but harvesting of building material.
Trigona / Plebeia sp. · Piper sp.
#004
August
Paper wasp
on Duranta / Sky Flower
Nectaring
Large social wasp working the small lavender flowers of Duranta. Wasps are underappreciated pollinators — this species also hunts caterpillars, making it a dual-role resident.
Duranta · 4 visitor spp. recorded
Polistes / Polybia sp. · Duranta erecta
#004
August
Tiger Longwing
on Ixora
Nectaring
One of the larger Heliconius — black wings with cream spots and an orange-rufous hindwing patch. Second butterfly species recorded on Ixora. Heliconius are long-lived and pollen-feeding as well as nectaring; their presence indicates a garden with enough complexity to support resident populations.
Ixora · 3 butterfly spp. recorded
Heliconius hecale · Ixora coccinea
#005
August
Striped leaf beetle
on Unidentified shrub
Grazing on leaf
Black elytra with cream longitudinal stripes. A herbivore, not a pollinator — and a likely prey item for the wasps and birds documented elsewhere in this log. Every trophic level is present in the garden.
Chrysomelidae sp. · Unidentified
#203
#006
August
Leafcutter ant
on Gravel path — carrying flower fragment
Foraging — harvesting bloom for fungus garden
Major worker carrying a freshly cut flower — petals and stamens intact — larger than its own head, across the gravel path. The fragment goes underground to feed a fungus colony the ant will never see the product of. Leafcutters are among the most significant herbivores in Neotropical gardens; a mature colony processes hundreds of kilograms of plant material annually. They have not solved the block and tackle problem. They have decided not to need to.
Atta sp. · Unidentified flower (possibly Duranta erecta)
August
Giant Swallowtail
on Ixora
Nectaring
The largest butterfly in this log, wings partially spread, abdomen dipped into the Ixora cluster. Third butterfly species on this one plant. Ixora is functioning as a keystone nectar source — the data is in the photographs.
Ixora · 3 butterfly spp. recorded
Papilio cresphontes / P. thoas · Ixora coccinea
#106
September
Robber fly
on Yellow flower buds
Hovering — predation, not pollination
Orange abdomen, black thorax, iridescent wings. Robber flies hunt other insects on the wing — including bees and wasps. Its presence over a flowering plant is not about the plant. It is hunting the pollinators the plant attracts.
Asilidae sp. (possibly Mallophora) · Unidentified
#006
September
Longwing butterfly
on Coatbuttons
Nectaring
Black wings with orange-red and white banding. The host is Tridax procumbens — a common garden weed. First of five visitor species documented on this plant.
Coatbuttons · 5 visitor spp. recorded
Heliconius sp. · Tridax procumbens
#101
September
Stingless bee
on Coatbuttons
Nectaring — face-on
Small meliponine bee buried face-first in the Tridax disc florets — the bee's orange abdomen and dark head visible from above. Fourth visitor species on this weed. The face-on view documents contact pollination directly.
Coatbuttons · 5 visitor spp. recorded
Meliponini sp. · Tridax procumbens
#102
September
Solitary bee
on Coatbuttons — seeding head
Pollen collecting
Black and white banded solitary bee working a Tridax head in seed. Fifth visitor species on this plant — and a different guild entirely from the social Meliponini. Solitary bees are among the most effective pollinators per individual. The weed is serving multiple bee communities simultaneously.
Coatbuttons · 5 visitor spp. recorded
Megachile sp. or similar · Tridax procumbens
#106
September
White butterfly
on Coatbuttons — dried seedhead
Resting
Perched on a dried, spent Tridax head — not nectaring but resting. The same plant serves as nectar source, landing platform, and seed dispersal structure. Three functions, one weed.
Coatbuttons · 5 visitor spp. recorded
Leptophobia aripa or similar Pieridae · Tridax procumbens
#109
September
Stingless bee
on Red zinnia
Hovering approach
Small orange-yellow bee suspended above the disc florets of a red zinnia, moment before landing. The frame documents the approach rather than the contact. Zinnias are primary meliponine forage.
Meliponini sp. · Zinnia elegans
#204
September
Banded Peacock butterfly
on Coatbuttons
Nectaring
Third butterfly species on Tridax procumbens. With five visitor species now documented, this weed is the most biodiverse plant in the log.
Coatbuttons · 5 visitor spp. recorded
Anartia fatima · Tridax procumbens
#026
September
Painted locust
on Bare ground
Basking — herbivore at rest
Large yellow-green grasshopper with black-spotted wings and orange antennae, on bare dry soil. No plant interaction recorded — but as a herbivore and prey species it belongs in the web. Grasshoppers of this size are taken by grackles, orioles, and large lizards. The garden feeds predators it never photographs.
Schistocerca melanocera or similar Acrididae · Ground level
#107
September
Dragonfly
on Dry twig — garden edge
Perching between sorties
Reddish-brown dragonfly perched on a bare twig over dry soil — a hunting perch, not a plant interaction. Dragonflies are aerial predators of mosquitoes, gnats, and small flies; their presence indicates the garden supports enough insect biomass to sustain a predator at this level. An indicator species for a functioning system.
Orthemis ferruginea or similar Libellulidae · Garden perch
#111
September
Hoffmann's Woodpecker
on Coconut palm
Excavating endosperm
The bird has breached the green husk of an attached coconut to reach the white endosperm and liquid cavity beneath. The cavity is deliberate — sustained work over multiple visits. The most unexpected interaction in this log.
Hoffmann's Woodpecker · 2 plant spp.
Melanerpes hoffmannii · Cocos nucifera
Cashew
Dry season
Streak-backed Oriole
on Cashew
Feeding on ripe cashew apple
Brilliant orange oriole with black mask and wings feeding on a fully ripe cashew apple. Two birds now documented on the cashew: the oriole feeds on the ripe fruit; the Red-lored Amazon (April) predates the immature nut. Same tree, opposite roles.
Cashew · 2 bird spp., opposite roles
Icterus pustulatus · Anacardium occidentale
Late Dry Season · December
#998
December
Hoffmann's Woodpecker
on Date palm
Foraging in infructescence
The same species as the coconut entry, three months later, working the ripening drupes of the date palm. The woodpecker appears twice in this log across two plant species and two seasons — a reminder that individual animals have territories and that the garden is part of them.
Hoffmann's Woodpecker · 2 plant spp.
Melanerpes hoffmannii · Phoenix sp.
#002
December
Mantled Howler Monkey
on Guanacaste tree / legume canopy
Browsing — largest vertebrate in the log
Adult male, full black coat with golden-brown mantle fur at the shoulder. Seated in a fine-leafed legume — possibly Guanacaste (Enterolobium cyclocarpum) — at the start of the dry season flush. Expression relaxed, gaze upward; resting between feeding bouts. Howlers are browsers and opportunistic frugivores, taking young leaves, flowers, and fruit by season. The largest vertebrate in this log and the only primate — their passage through the garden is a measure of how the property sits within a larger corridor.
Alouatta palliata · Enterolobium cyclocarpum (tentative)
El Oasis · Garden Field Log · 32 interactions · Playa Hermosa, Guanacaste
